passages.title: Screen time and reading scores
passages.body:
A county reported that schools where students averaged higher daily screen time for leisure also had lower average scores on a standardized reading test. Officials proposed a district policy limiting recreational screen use on school-issued devices.

Analysts pointed out that schools in wealthier areas both limited leisure screen access more strictly and funded more library programming. In schools with crowded housing, students often relied on shared devices and longer sessions simply because fewer gadgets were available at home.

questions.prompt: The analysts’ comments suggest that raw correlation between screen time and reading scores could partly reflect —
choices: students in high-screen schools reading more novels
the reading test becoming easier in wealthy areas
the colour of school walls
confounding community resources and home device access rather than screens alone harming reading
questions.answer: confounding community resources and home device access rather than screens alone harming reading

questions.prompt: For the officials’ policy proposal to follow logically from the correlation described, they must implicitly treat leisure screen time as —
choices: impossible to measure
a driver of lower scores rather than a side effect of other conditions
unrelated to reading scores
beneficial when shared among siblings
questions.answer: a driver of lower scores rather than a side effect of other conditions

questions.prompt: Which evidence would best help decide whether cutting leisure screen time on school devices improves reading scores?
choices: The number of apps installed on each tablet
Average cafeteria lunch prices across schools
Opinions from device manufacturers about durability
A trial where similar schools randomly receive stricter leisure limits while others do not, then comparing reading growth
questions.answer: A trial where similar schools randomly receive stricter leisure limits while others do not, then comparing reading growth

questions.prompt: Statement: “Every student who completed the optional summer workbook passed the fall review.” Alex did not pass the fall review. What follows?
choices: Alex must have completed the workbook.
No one completed the workbook.
The workbook was too short.
Alex did not complete the optional summer workbook
questions.answer: Alex did not complete the optional summer workbook

questions.prompt: Company memo: “We doubled sales this quarter; therefore our new slogan caused the jump.” The flaw in this reasoning is similar to blaming —
choices: gravity for objects falling
oven heat for baking bread
a rooster’s crow for the sunrise
an umbrella sales spike on rainy weather
questions.answer: a rooster’s crow for the sunrise

questions.prompt: Which option best fills the gap? “The witness’s memory of the licence plate has been inconsistent across interviews, so we should treat that detail as —”
choices: definitive proof of guilt
more accurate than video footage
irrelevant to the investigation
less reliable unless corroborated by independent evidence
questions.answer: less reliable unless corroborated by independent evidence

questions.prompt: Two editorials use the same statistics on youth volunteering. One says young people are unusually selfish; the other says they are remarkably engaged. The contrast best illustrates that —
choices: statistics cannot be copied between articles
numbers alone do not force a single interpretation; framing matters
volunteering data are always fabricated
editorials may not discuss youth
questions.answer: numbers alone do not force a single interpretation; framing matters

questions.prompt: Plan: “To reduce litter, we will add more bins; bins always eliminate litter because people dislike carrying trash.” Which question most directly tests a hidden assumption?
choices: What colour the bins should be painted
How many letters are in the word litter
Whether people will use new bins if they are placed where litter actually accumulates
Whether bins existed anywhere in the world before 1900
questions.answer: Whether people will use new bins if they are placed where litter actually accumulates

questions.prompt: Nadia: “We should pick the trail with the gentler slope so nobody twists an ankle.” Omar: “The steep trail is shorter, so we’ll finish before dark.” Their discussion mainly contrasts —
choices: favourite colours of hiking boots
safety risk versus time saved
types of trees found on each trail
whether hiking should exist

questions.prompt: Argument: “This painting sold for a record price; therefore it is the finest work in the gallery.” The most precise critique is that price —
choices: always equals artistic quality by definition
is never reported at auctions
is illegal to discuss in public
reflects many factors (fashion, scarcity, bidding) beyond a simple ranking of quality
questions.answer: reflects many factors (fashion, scarcity, bidding) beyond a simple ranking of quality
